## EC 4710: Computer Hardware Design - Course Overview

### 1. Course Identification & Staff
*   **Course Name:** EC 4710 Computer Hardware Design
*   **Instructor:** Professor [Name not specified in transcript]
    *   **Contact:** Information available on screen during lecture.
    *   **Office Hours:** Virtual, Tuesdays 2:00 PM - 4:00 PM, flexible by request.
*   **Teaching Assistants (TAs):** Staff listed, but may change.

### 2. Lecture Format
*   **Live Synchronous Sessions:** Thursdays (focus on examples, less theory; exception: first lecture).
*   **Asynchronous Sessions:** Tuesdays (pre-recorded video lectures).

### 3. Laboratory Information
*   **Number of Sections:** Four laboratory sections.
*   **Online Section:** Only one specific section (007 or 15707) is designated online. All others are scheduled face-to-face.
*   **Face-to-Face Lab Start Date:** University (OU) policy dictates face-to-face labs begin in the third week of the semester.

## **Lecture Summary: Course Overview & Expectations**

### **I. Technical Requirements & Software**

*   **Remote Desktop Service:** Can be used for homework, but **not for programming the board.**
*   **Local Software Installation:** Essential for programming the board.
    *   Need Vivado software installed on your computer.
    *   A reliable internet connection is mandatory.
*   **Board Usage:** You will work with the board *a lot*, repeatedly, throughout the semester, not just as a final step. This differs significantly from previous courses (e.g., EC 2700).
*   **Backup Plan:**
    *   Always have a backup for your files (e.g., Dropbox, Google Drive). OU students may have Google Drive access.
    *   Your computer can fail; losing data is a major risk.

### **II. Course Topics & Content**

The course is divided into several key areas, balancing design, theory, and practical application.

*   **Digital Systems Design (Initial Focus):**
    *   Review of simple examples (some may be familiar).
    *   Focus on designing digital systems and writing VHDL code.
    *   **Key Concept:** VHDL (Very High Speed Integrated Circuit Hardware Description Language).
*   **Computer Arithmetic (Challenging Section):**
    *   **Unsigned Numbers:** Briefly reviewed.
    *   **Fixed-Point Arithmetic:** Covered in detail.
    *   **Floating-Point Arithmetic:** Covered in detail; often a struggle point for students.
    *   **Purpose:** Used to represent real numbers.
*   **External Peripherals Design (Fun but Challenging):**
    *   Covers communication protocols and hardware interfaces.
    *   **Examples:**
        *   SPI protocol (e.g., accelerometer communication).
        *   I2C protocol (e.g., temperature sensor communication).
        *   PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) for control (e.g., RGB LED intensity, audio frequencies).
        *   PDM (Pulse-Density Modulation) for microphones (e.g., recording and playback of voice).
    *   **Challenge:** Peripherals are numerous and unique, leading to complex digital systems and state machine designs.
*   **Special Circuit Design Techniques:**
    *   **CORDIC Algorithm:** For computing trigonometric and transcendental functions.
    *   **Other Units:** Square roots, BCDR, CSA (Carry-Save Adder), multi-tier CSA.
    *   **Purpose:** Highly specific units helpful for advanced design.
*   **FPGA Resources:**
    *   Configurable Logic Block (CLB).
    *   FIFO (First-In, First-Out) buffers.
    *   Block RAM (distinct from simple RAM).
    *   DSP (Digital Signal Processing) units (multiplication and addition).
    *   Clock Managers (e.g., professional frequency division).
    *   XADCs (e.g., Analog-to-Digital Converters within the FPGA).
*   **Pipelining:**
    *   Technique to increase operational speed.
    *   One session dedicated to this topic.
*   **Microprocessor Design (Very Fun Section):**
    *   Covers three or four simple microprocessor designs.
    *   Includes all necessary components.
*   **Advanced VHDL Topics:**
    *   Building parametric circuits.
    *   Writing and reading text files for simulation and synthesis.
    *   Embedding counters and registers inside ASM (Advanced State Machine) – an advanced topic.

### **III. Class Structure & Online Learning**

*   **Online Semester Structure:**
    *   Lectures have a defined structure, outlining what will be covered in each.
    *   **Synchronous Lectures (Grey Shaded):** Live Zoom sessions.
        *   Today's topics: Class policy, structure, and digital system design.
        *   **Recommendation:** Attend synchronous sessions to avoid videos piling up.
    *   **Asynchronous Videos (Red Shaded):** Posted instead of a Tuesday session.
*   **Communication:**
    *   Feel free to interrupt with questions during lectures.
    *   Use the Zoom chat feature for questions if preferred.
*   **Technical Assistance (Zoom/Moodle):**
    *   Contact computer support for these issues. The instructor may not be able to resolve them.

### **IV. Assignments & Grading**

*   **Homework & Exams:**
    *   PDFs will be posted; solutions must be submitted as PDFs by the due date.
    *   Methods for generating PDFs are flexible (direct editing, printing and scanning).
*   **Lab Work:**
    *   **Individual:** Labs are *not* group assignments.
    *   **Crucial:** Labs constitute **36% of the entire grade**.
    *   **Failure Condition:** Must achieve **60% in labs to pass the class**.
    *   **Warning:** Do not get behind on labs; there is a lot of work.
    *   Specific due dates for each section are provided with the lab.
*   **Final Project:**
    *   **Group Work:** 2 to 4 people per group.
    *   **Group Formation:** Students are expected to form their own groups. Instructor will assign randomly only if students contact him without a group.
    *   **Failure Condition:** Not participating in the final project results in failing the class.

### **V. Policies & Important Notes**

*   **Email Communication:**
    *   **Strict Policy:** Instructor and TAs will **only reply to emails from OU accounts.**
    *   **Reason:** To verify sender identity and avoid FERPA violations, especially when discussing grades.
*   **Academic Conduct Policy:**

## Lecture Summary: Abstraction Layers in Computer Systems Design

### I. Class Logistics & Initial Questions

*   **Dropping the Class:** Discussion initiated, if necessary.
*   **Lab Access:**
    *   No prior request needed for access.
    *   Access restricted to **scheduled lab times only** due to COVID protocols.
    *   A TA will always be present during scheduled lab times.
    *   The building is expected to be open (tentative date: 19th).
    *   Previously (pre-COVID), students had free access to labs at any time.

### II. Introduction to Digital System Design and Context

*   **First Unit:** "Digital System Design" posted in Moodle.
*   **Core Question:** Why learn digital system design (machines, diagrams, VHDL) when similar functionalities can be achieved with simpler tools like Arduino? What is its purpose and utility?
*   **Main Topic:** "Construction Layers in Computer Systems Design" – exploring different methods to implement a digital system.

### III. Implementation Approaches: A Hierarchy of Abstraction Layers

The lecture uses the example of a **Face Recognition System** to illustrate different implementation strategies.

1.  **High-Level Software (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink)**
    *   **Method:** Writing code in high-level languages or using graphical programming tools.
    *   **Characteristics:**
        *   **MATLAB:** Easy to use, no explicit variable declaration, quick compilation/execution.
        *   **Simulink:** Drag-and-drop pre-built components for system creation.
    *   **Advantages:**
        *   **Fastest Development:** Rapid prototyping and implementation.
        *   **Ease of Use:** Low barrier to entry for programmers.
    *   **Disadvantages:**
        *   **Slower Execution Speed:** Programs run slower than lower-level implementations.
        *   **Higher Memory Consumption:** Less efficient use of memory.

2.  **Mid-Level Software (e.g., C/C++)**
    *   **Method:** Writing code in compiled languages.
    *   **Characteristics:** More challenging than MATLAB.
    *   **Advantages:**
        *   **Faster Program Execution:** In theory, a well-written C/C++ program runs faster than MATLAB.
    *   **Disadvantages:**
        *   **Requires More Skillful Programming:** Demands deeper programming knowledge.
        *   **Longer Development Time:** Takes more time to develop and debug.

3.  **Embedded C (Microcontrollers/Microprocessors)**
    *   **Method:** Programming in C for small microprocessors (e.g., Arduino).
    *   **Characteristics:** Requires even more skillful programming techniques.
    *   **Perceived "Best Shot":** Often considered the optimal implementation by many due to a balance of control and ease.

4.  **Hardware Description Languages (HDLs) - Digital System Design (Register Transfer Level - RTL)**
    *   **Method:** Directly designing the digital system using HDLs like VHDL or Verilog, at the level of registers, counters, multiplexers.
    *   **Characteristics:** Far more challenging than software development. This is the focus of the class.
    *   **Advantages:**
        *   **Significantly Faster Execution:** Systems can run *much* faster.
        *   **Lower Memory Consumption:** Highly efficient use of resources.
        *   **Lower Power Consumption:** Critical for many applications (e.g., battery-powered devices).
    *   **Disadvantages:**
        *   **Much More Difficult:** Requires specialized knowledge in digital design, VHDL/Verilog, and simulation.
        *   **Longer Development & Verification Time:** Complex to design and verify correctly.

5.  **Logic Gate Level**
    *   **Method:** Designing systems using pure logic gates (AND, OR, NOT, etc.).
    *   **Characteristics:** Even more annoying and complex than RTL, but possible.

6.  **Transistor Level**
    *   **Method:** Designing systems at the fundamental transistor level.
    *   **Characteristics:**
        *   Extremely specialized skill.
        *   Requires significant financial investment (expensive engineers, specialized tools).
        *   Current microprocessors are typically designed at this level (often by specialized teams).
    *   **Advantages:**
        *   **Ultimate Performance:** Provides the absolute best performance for the system.

### IV. Choosing the Right Implementation: When & Why

*   **It Depends:** The choice of implementation layer depends heavily on specific requirements, project constraints, and what the company values most (e.g., speed, power, cost, development time).
*   **Trade-offs:** There is a continuous trade-off between:
    *   **Development Time/Effort** (easier/faster at higher abstraction)
    *   **Performance (Speed, Power, Memory)** (better at lower abstraction)
    *   **Cost** (lower for simpler software, higher for custom hardware)
    *   **Skill Level Required** (higher for lower abstraction)

**Examples:**

1.  **Christmas Lights (Simple Application):**
    *   **Choice:** Arduino (Embedded C/Software).
    *   **Reason:** Simplicity, ease of programming, quick deployment. No need for complex VHDL design.
2.  **Video Codec (H.265 for Netflix):**
    *   **Problem:** Pure software (MATLAB/C) implementations don't meet performance requirements.
    *   **Choice:** **ASIC** (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) – pure hardware (VHDL/transistor level).
    *   **Reason:** Essential for achieving the necessary speed for video compression/decompression. Often implemented as a dedicated chip.
3.  **Temperature Sensor Chip (e.g., for Nexus Board) & I2C Interface:**
    *   **Scenario:** Designing a chip to be sold in millions.
    *   **Choice:** Pure hardware (VHDL/Verilog) for the I2C interface and other digital components.
    *   **Reason:**
        *   Cost-effectiveness: Avoids integrating an entire expensive microcontroller (like an ATmega) into every tiny sensor chip.
        *   Efficiency: Implement only the necessary small piece of logic for the interface.
    *   **Key Insight:** Even within microcontrollers like Arduino, the I2C (and other peripheral) logic is implemented in **pure hardware**, not software, for optimal performance and efficiency.

### V. Key Concepts

*   **Construction/Abstraction Layers:** Different levels at which a digital system can be designed and implemented, from high-level software to low-level transistors.
*   **MATLAB/Simulink:** High-level software for rapid prototyping, but slower execution.
*   **C/C++:** Mid-level software offering better performance than MATLAB, but requiring more skill.
*   **Embedded C:** Programming for microcontrollers, balancing control and ease.
*   **Digital System Design (RTL):** Designing hardware using VHDL/Verilog for speed, power, and memory efficiency.
*   **VHDL/Verilog:** Hardware Description Languages used for designing digital circuits.
*   **Logic Gate Level:** Implementation using basic logic gates.
*   **Transistor Level:** Fundamental hardware design for ultimate performance.
*   **ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit):** Custom-designed hardware chip for a specific function, offering maximum performance and efficiency.
*   **I2C Interface:** A common serial communication protocol, often implemented in hardware within chips.
*   **Trade-offs:** The inherent compromises between performance, development time, cost, and complexity in system design.

### VI. Important Takeaways

*   There are multiple valid ways to implement any given application, ranging from high-level software to low-level hardware.
*   The optimal choice of implementation method depends entirely on the specific application's requirements, such as performance, power consumption, memory footprint, development time, and cost.
*   While software development can generally be quicker and easier, complex or high-performance applications often *necessitate* hardware design to meet critical specifications.
*   Learning digital system design (e.g., VHDL at RTL) provides the skills to create highly optimized, fast, and power-efficient systems that are not achievable through software alone.
*   Understanding these abstraction layers is crucial for engineers to make informed design decisions.

## Exam-Ready Summary

### Abstraction Layers in Computer Systems Design

This topic explores the various methods of implementing digital systems, from high-level software to fundamental hardware, and the critical factors influencing the choice between them.

**1. The Spectrum of Implementation Layers:**

*   **Software (High-Level to Embedded):**
    *   **MATLAB/Simulink:** Easiest, fastest to develop. **Disadvantage:** Slowest execution, highest memory usage.
    *   **C/C++:** More challenging than MATLAB. **Advantage:** Faster execution. **Disadvantage:** More complex, longer development time.
    *   **Embedded C (Microcontrollers):** Requires skillful programming. Often a balance, but still software-based.
*   **Hardware (HDL to Transistor):**
    *   **Digital System Design (RTL via VHDL/Verilog):** Significantly more challenging than software. **Key Advantages:** **Much faster execution, lower memory, lower power consumption.** This is the core skill taught in this course.
    *   **Logic Gate Level:** Possible, but very complex.
    *   **Transistor Level:** Most complex, highest specialized skill. **Key Advantage:** **Absolute best performance.** Used for modern microprocessors.

**2. Why Choose Hardware (Digital System Design)?**

Hardware implementations, particularly at the Register Transfer Level (RTL) using VHDL/Verilog, are chosen for applications requiring:

*   **Extreme Speed:** When software cannot meet real-time processing demands (e.g., video codecs like H.265).
*   **Low Power Consumption:** Critical for battery-powered devices.
*   **Minimal Memory Footprint:** Efficient use of resources in embedded systems or integrated circuits.
*   **Dedicated Functionality:** Creating application-specific integrated circuits (ASICs) for specific, high-volume tasks (e.g., I2C interfaces within a sensor chip).

**3. The "It Depends" Principle & Trade-offs:**

The decision between software and hardware is not about one being inherently "better" but rather about **trade-offs** based on project requirements:

*   **Software is favored when:**
    *   Rapid development and ease of programming are paramount (e.g., simple Christmas lights control).
    *   Performance requirements are not extremely stringent.
    *   Flexibility and ease of updates are important.
*   **Hardware is favored when:**
    *   **Performance (speed, power, memory) is a critical, non-negotiable constraint.**
    *   High-volume production demands cost-optimized and highly efficient dedicated chips.
    *   Specialized functions are required that software cannot execute efficiently enough.

**4. Key Concepts to Remember:**

*   **Abstraction Layers:** Understand the hierarchy from high-level software down to transistors.
*   **VHDL/Verilog:** Hardware Description Languages for designing at the RTL.
*   **ASIC:** Application-Specific Integrated Circuit – a custom chip designed for ultimate performance in a specific task.
*   **Performance Metrics:** Speed, power consumption, and memory usage are key differentiators between layers.
*   **Development Cost vs. Run-time Performance:** Higher abstraction (software) means faster development but lower run-time performance; lower abstraction (hardware) means slower development but superior run-time performance.

**Exam Focus:** Be prepared to explain the advantages and disadvantages of different implementation layers, and *justify* why a particular layer would be chosen for a given application or constraint (e.g., why a video codec needs hardware, but Christmas lights don't).

## Digital System Design: Abstraction Levels, Architecture, and Best Practices

### 1. Levels of Abstraction in System Design

This section outlines the different levels of abstraction in designing digital systems, moving from low-level hardware to high-level software.

#### 1.1 Hardware Implementation Levels

*   **Register Transfer Level (RTL):**
    *   **Description:** A common and preferred level for digital system design.
    *   **Components:** Utilizes fundamental hardware components such as registers, multiplexers, decoders, and shift registers.
    *   **Industry Relevance:** Frequently asked about in interviews (e.g., "Have you ever thrown in an RTL?").
*   **Micro-architecture Level:**
    *   **Description:** The system level for hardware, involving the implementation and interconnection of various functional blocks.
    *   **Focus:** Designing how a microprocessor or other complex hardware components operate at a block level.

#### 1.2 Software Implementation Levels

*   **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) Level:**
    *   **Description:** Marks the transition from hardware-centric design to software.
    *   **Focus:** Designing the specific set of instructions a microprocessor will understand and execute to achieve optimal application performance.
*   **Operating System (OS) Level:**
    *   **Description:** Ranges from bare-bones to highly complex operating systems.
    *   **Trade-off:** Smaller OS are generally faster but more complex to develop and manage.
*   **Programming Language Level:**
    *   **Description:** Involves different tiers of programming languages (e.g., C, Python, MATLAB).
    *   **Note:** Some tools like MATLAB are sometimes considered interpreters rather than full programming languages.
*   **Algorithm Level:**
    *   **Description:** The ideal, highest level of abstraction, where one could theoretically simply specify an algorithm and have the system implement it automatically.
    *   **Status:** Not fully realized in practice yet.

#### 1.3 Course Focus and Overall Perspective

*   **Course Coverage:** This class primarily focuses on the **Register Transfer Level (RTL)** and **Micro-architecture Level**. Some aspects of **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** design are also touched upon.
*   **Interconnectedness:** These levels are not monolithic but can be mixed and matched; however, this classification provides a useful framework for understanding digital system implementation.

### 2. Core Digital System Architecture

#### 2.1 The System Model

*   **Fundamental Structure:** A typical digital system consists of one (or more) **state machine** controlling a **data path circuit**.
*   **Data Path Circuit:**
    *   **Function:** Performs the actual data manipulation and processing.
    *   **Complexity:** Can vary greatly in size and complexity, potentially containing many components, including other nested state machines.
    *   **Interactions:** Inputs can feed both the state machine and the data path. Outputs from the data path can influence the state machine, and the state machine dictates the data path's operations.

#### 2.2 Key Concepts for Digital System Design

To effectively design digital systems, students need to understand:

*   **Finite State Machines (FSMs):**
    *   **Emphasis:** Mastery of **Algorithmic State Machine (ASM) diagrams** for FSM representation.
*   **Component Interconnection:** How to properly connect various digital components (e.g., multiplexers, decoders, registers).
*   **Hierarchical Design:** Building complex systems by combining and organizing many smaller components.
*   **Component Description:** How to describe and implement these pieces using Hardware Description Languages (HDL).
*   **Data Path Circuit Utilization:** Prior knowledge of how to use and design data path circuits is a prerequisite.

### 3. Common Components and Design Practices

#### 3.1 Essential Data Path Components

Common and frequently used components in digital system design include:

*   **Counters:** Crucial for sequencing and timing operations.
*   **Registers:** Store data; includes basic registers, shift registers, and parallel access shift registers.
*   **Multiplexers (Muxes):** Select one of several input signals and forward it to a single output.
*   **Decoders:** Convert binary information from N inputs to a maximum of 2^N unique outputs.
*   **Arithmetic Units:** Perform arithmetic operations (e.g., adders, multipliers).

#### 3.2 Utilizing Pre-designed Parametric VHDL Components

*   **Best Practice:** It is highly recommended to use **pre-designed VHDL components** for common elements like counters and registers, rather than writing them from scratch repeatedly.
*   **Benefits:**
    *   **Efficiency:** Saves significant development time.
    *   **Reliability:** Reduces the introduction of errors by using already tested and verified components.
*   **Provided Parametric Components (Examples):**
    *   `my_reg_E`: An N-bit register with enabled and asynchronous clear.
    *   `my_counter_mod_N`: A modulo N counter with enabled and synchronous clear.
    *   `my_DA_shift_reg`: A parallel access shift register (directional, enabled, synchronous clear).
*   **Parametric Design:** These components are **parametric**, meaning their characteristics (e.g., bit width for a register, modulo N for a counter) can be configured using **generic parameters** in VHDL.
    *   **Implementation:** `generic map` in VHDL is used to set these parameters (refer to VHDL for FPGA tutorial #7).
*   **Resource:** These components are available for download in the course tutorials.

### 4. Examples of Digital Systems

#### 4.1 Car Load Counter

*   **Functionality:** Detects cars entering or leaving a parking lot and updates a count.
*   **Components:**
    *   **State Machine:** Interprets sequences from a photoreceptor to determine if a car is entering or leaving.
    *   **Data Path Circuit:** A simple counter that increments (car enters) or decrements (car leaves).
*   **Representation:** The state machine is typically designed using **ASM diagrams**.

#### 4.2 Debouncing Circuit

*   **Problem:** Mechanical buttons and switches produce noisy, "bouncing" signals (fluctuations between 0 and 1) when pressed or released, rather than clean transitions.
*   **Goal:** To filter this noisy input into a stable, ideal digital signal.
*   **Filtering Approach:**
    *   If the input signal remains constant at '1' for a minimum duration (e.g., 20 milliseconds), assign the output as '1'.
    *   If the input signal remains constant at '0' for a minimum duration (e.g., 20 milliseconds), assign the output as '0'.
*   **Implementation:**
    *   **State Machine:** Controls the overall debouncing logic, monitoring the input and triggering actions.
    *   **Data Path Circuit:** Typically includes a **modular counter** with a **comparator** to measure the constant duration of the input signal.

---

### Key Concepts

*   **Register Transfer Level (RTL):** Design using registers, muxes, decoders.
*   **Micro-architecture Level:** System-level hardware design.
*   **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA):** Software interface to hardware.
*   **Finite State Machine (FSM):** Controls circuit behavior based on states and inputs.
*   **Algorithmic State Machine (ASM) Diagram:** A specific method for designing FSMs.
*   **Data Path Circuit:** Performs data processing and manipulation.
*   **Hierarchical Design:** Building systems from reusable components.
*   **Parametric VHDL Components:** Reusable VHDL modules configurable via generic parameters (e.g., `generic map`).
*   **Debouncing:** Filtering mechanical switch noise to produce clean digital signals.

### Important Takeaways

1.  **Understand the Hierarchy:** Recognizing the different levels of abstraction (hardware to software) is fundamental for effective system design and understanding a project's scope.
2.  **FSMs and Data Paths are Core:** Almost all digital systems can be conceptualized as a state machine controlling a data path circuit. Mastering this interaction is critical.
3.  **ASM Diagrams are Essential:** Learn and consistently use ASM diagrams for designing state machines, especially in this course.
4.  **Embrace Reusability:** Do not re-invent the wheel. Leverage pre-designed, tested, and parametric VHDL components (like counters, registers, shift registers) to improve design efficiency and reduce errors.
5.  **Practical Applications:** Even seemingly simple systems like car counters and debouncers require sophisticated digital design principles involving FSMs and data paths.
6.  **VHDL Skills:** Familiarity with VHDL constructs, particularly `generic map` for customizing parametric components, is crucial for practical implementation.

### Exam-Ready Summary

Digital system design involves multiple abstraction levels, from low-level hardware (RTL, Micro-architecture) to high-level software (ISA, OS, Programming Language, Algorithm). This course emphasizes RTL and Micro-architecture. A core principle is designing systems as a **state machine** (often represented by **ASM diagrams**) controlling a **data path circuit**. Essential knowledge includes FSMs, component interconnection, and **hierarchical design**. Best practices advocate using **pre-designed, parametric VHDL components** (e.g., counters, registers) for common functions to enhance efficiency and reduce design errors. These parametric components are customized using `generic map`. Real-world examples like car load counters and debouncing circuits effectively illustrate the application of state machines and data paths to solve practical problems.

Here's a structured summary of the lecture transcript, including headings, bullet points, key concepts, important takeaways, and an exam-ready summary:

---

### Lecture Summary: Digital System Design & Components

#### 1. Fundamental Component Review

*   **Initial Signal State:** At the beginning of an operation, the signal is often set to zero.
*   **Counter Management:**
    *   The counter is cleared (set to zero) at the start of a process.
    *   It is incremented every time a specific condition is met.
*   **Crucial Knowledge:** It is essential to understand how fundamental digital components work, specifically:
    *   **Counters:** How they are enabled, cleared, and incremented.
    *   **Registers:** How they store and process data.
    *   **Shift Registers:** How they load data (e.g., parallel load) and perform shifts (e.g., right shift).
*   **Resource:** Refer to previous class notes (e.g., "notes seven" or "Generic components behavior") for a refresher on these components' operations and their behavior on clock ticks. Lack of this foundational knowledge can lead to significant issues in system design.

#### 2. Debouncing Circuit Mechanism

*   **Purpose:** To filter out noisy or fluctuating input signals (like from a button press) and produce a stable, "debounced" output.
*   **Operation (Illustrative Example for a '1' signal):**
    1.  **Initial State:** Signal is zero. Clear the counter.
    2.  **Wait for First '1':** Once the input signal (`W`) goes high, start counting.
    3.  **Count Duration of '1':**
        *   If `W` remains '1' for *less than* a specified duration (e.g., 20 milliseconds), the counter is reset, and the system returns to waiting for a stable '1'. The debounced output (`W_DV`) remains zero.
        *   If `W` remains '1' for *at least* 20 milliseconds, the input is considered stable, and the debounced output (`W_DV`) is set to '1'.
    4.  **Wait for First '0':** Once `W_DV` is '1', the system then waits for the input signal (`W`) to go low.
    5.  **Count Duration of '0':**
        *   If `W` remains '0' for *less than* 20 milliseconds, `W_DV` remains '1'.
        *   If `W` remains '0' for *at least* 20 milliseconds, `W_DV` is set to '0', and the process restarts from step 1.
*   **Control:** This entire process is typically governed by an Algorithmic State Machine (ASM) diagram.

#### 3. Bit Counting Circuit: Algorithm to Digital System Design

*   **Problem Statement:** Design a digital system to count the number of '1's (set bits) in a given sequence of bits (e.g., an 8-bit input `A`).
*   **Sequential Algorithm:**
    *   Initialize a `counter` to 0.
    *   **While** the input `A` is not zero:
        *   Check the Least Significant Bit (LSB) of `A`.
        *   **If** LSB is '1', increment the `counter`.
        *   Right-shift `A` by one position (`A = A >> 1`).
    *   The loop continues until all bits of `A` have been processed (i.e., `A` becomes 0).
*   **Digital System Design Methodology:**
    1.  **High-Level State Machine (HLSM):** Start by conceptualizing the system's states and transitions based on the algorithm.
        *   Example states: Initialization (counter=0), Wait for Start, Process Data (checking LSB, shifting, incrementing), Done (outputs ready).
    2.  **General System Input/Output (I/O) Conventions:**
        *   `Start` signal: Initiates the system's operation (e.g., pressing a button).
        *   `Down` signal: Indicates that the system's outputs (e.g., the `count`) are ready and valid.
    3.  **ASM Diagram & Data Path Implementation:** Translate the HLSM into a detailed ASM chart. This involves:
        *   **Control Unit (ASM):** Defines the sequence of operations and generates control signals.
        *   **Data Path:** Comprises components that store and process data according to the control signals.
            *   **Parallel Access Shift Register:** To load the initial 8-bit input `A` and perform right shifts.
            *   **Counter:** To store and increment the `count` of '1's.
            *   **Comparator:** To check if `A` is zero (loop termination condition).
            *   **LSB Extractor:** To get `A[0]` (the Least Significant Bit).
    4.  **Example Control Signals:**
        *   `Clear` signal for the counter.
        *   `Load` signal for the shift register (to input `A`).
        *   `Shift Enable` for the shift register.
        *   `Increment Enable` for the counter.
        *   These signals are generated by the state machine based on the current state and conditions.
*   **Complexity:** Designing digital systems from algorithms is a skill that requires practice and exposure to various examples; it is not always a straightforward process.

#### 4. Importance of Timing Diagrams & Debugging

*   **Necessity:** Understanding and completing timing diagrams is crucial.
*   **Debugging Tool:** For complex circuits encountered in this course, timing diagrams are indispensable for proper simulation and debugging.
*   **Methodology:** You cannot simply build a complex circuit and hope to find errors on a physical board; careful simulation using timing diagrams is required.
*   **Application:** This skill will be applied starting from early labs (e.g., Lab Number 1).

---

### Key Concepts

*   **Counters, Registers, Shift Registers:** Fundamental building blocks of digital systems.
*   **Enabled/Clear Signals:** Control inputs for component operation.
*   **Debouncing:** Process of stabilizing noisy digital inputs.
*   **Algorithmic State Machine (ASM) Diagram:** A flowchart-like method for describing sequential logic, used for both control and data path interactions.
*   **High-Level State Machine (HLSM):** An initial, abstract representation of system behavior.
*   **Data Path:** The functional units (registers, ALUs, counters, etc.) that process data.
*   **Control Unit:** The part of a digital system (often an ASM) that dictates the operations of the data path.
*   **System I/O Conventions:** Standard signals like `Start` (initiate) and `Down` (output valid) for system interaction.
*   **Timing Diagrams:** Visual representations of signal changes over time, critical for analysis and debugging.

---

### Important Takeaways

*   **Master Component Basics:** A deep understanding of how basic components (counters, registers, shift registers) operate, including their control signals (enable, clear, load, shift), is non-negotiable for success in digital design.
*   **ASM Diagrams are Foundational:** ASM diagrams are the primary tool for designing and understanding the sequential logic of digital systems and will be used extensively throughout the course.
*   **Systematic Design Process:** Convert algorithms into digital systems by first drafting a high-level state machine, then translating it into a detailed ASM diagram that interacts with a well-defined data path.
*   **Debugging Requires Simulation:** For complex circuits, hands-on debugging alone is insufficient. Meticulous simulation using tools like timing diagrams is essential to identify and rectify errors.
*   **Practice is Key:** Digital system design is a skill developed through practice and exposure to various design examples.

---

### Exam-Ready Summary

This lecture emphasizes the systematic design of digital systems from algorithms, starting with fundamental component knowledge and progressing to complex design methodologies.

**Core Principles:**

1.  **Component Mastery:** Essential to understand basic components (counters, registers, shift registers) and their control (enabled, clear, load, shift). Refer to notes (e.g., Notes 7) for detailed behavior.
2.  **ASM-Driven Design:** Algorithmic State Machine (ASM) diagrams are central to controlling sequential logic and will be used throughout the course for designing control units.

**Design Methodology (Algorithm to Digital System):**

1.  **Algorithm Definition:** Clearly define the sequential steps (e.g., bit counting algorithm: initialize counter, loop while `A != 0`, check LSB, increment counter, right shift `A`).
2.  **High-Level State Machine (HLSM):** Translate the algorithm into a conceptual state machine with states like "Initialize," "Wait for Start," "Process Data," and "Done."
3.  **Standard I/O:** Implement common system control signals: `Start` to initiate processing and `Down` to indicate output validity.
4.  **ASM & Data Path Implementation:**
    *   **Data Path:** Select appropriate components (e.g., parallel access shift register for loading/shifting, counter for accumulating results, comparator for condition checks like `A == 0`).
    *   **Control Unit (ASM):** Design an ASM diagram that generates control signals (e.g., `Clear`, `Load`, `Shift Enable`, `Increment Enable`) based on system states and input conditions (e.g., `Start==1`, `A==0`, `LSB==1`).
    *   This integrated approach links the high-level algorithm to the low-level hardware control.

**Debugging & Validation:**

*   **Timing Diagrams:** Crucial for simulating and debugging complex circuits. It is mandatory to understand how to complete and interpret them.
*   **Simulation over Guesswork:** For complex designs, thorough simulation using timing diagrams is required before physical implementation to effectively identify and correct issues.

**Example Applications:**

*   **Debouncing Circuit:** Demonstrates how an ASM with a counter can stabilize noisy inputs (e.g., ensuring a signal is stable for 20ms before acknowledging a state change).
*   **Bit Counting Circuit:** Illustrates the complete design flow from a sequential algorithm to a hardware implementation using an ASM, shift register, and counter.

**Key takeaway:** Digital system design is a systematic process requiring strong foundational knowledge of components, a clear design methodology using ASM diagrams, and effective debugging skills through simulation.

Here's a structured summary of the lecture transcript, focusing on the requested elements:

---

## Digital System Design Examples

### I. Integer Base Two Logarithm Algorithm

#### A. Key Concept
*   **Purpose:** Calculate the integer part of the base-2 logarithm of a number (A).
*   **Relationship:** Very similar in approach to a bit-counting circuit.

#### B. Sequential Algorithm
1.  Initialize output `L = 0`.
2.  **While `A` is not equal to `1`:**
    *   Update `A = A / 2` (truncate, round down, or floor division).
    *   Increment `L` by `1`.
3.  The final value of `L` is the integer base two logarithm.

#### C. Digital System Implementation
*   **Inputs:** `dA` (input data for A), `S` (start signal), `L` (output logarithm), `down` (valid signal).
*   **Components:**
    *   **Counter for `L`:** To increment the logarithm value.
    *   **Register for `A`:** To hold and update the input number.
    *   **Arithmetic Logic:**
        *   **Division by 2:** Implemented efficiently by a bit right-shift operation (e.g., `A3 A2 A1 A0` becomes `0 A3 A2 A1`). This is referred to as the "Florian operator" (floor division).
        *   **A minus A/2:** While conceptually a subtractor, the division by 2 is primarily a shift.
    *   **Multiplexer:** To select between loading initial `dA` into register `A` or routing the updated `A` (from the A/2 operation) back to the register.
    *   **Comparator for `A == 1`:**
        *   Checks if the current value of `A` is `1` to terminate the loop.
        *   **Optimization:** Can be simplified by checking if `A / 2` (after one shift) is equal to `0`, or by using a "null gate" (e.g., a NOR gate across the relevant bits).
*   **Finite State Machine (FSM) Control:**
    *   **State 0 (Initial):** Set `L = 0`.
    *   **State 1 (Load):** Load `dA` into register `A` when the `S` (start) signal is `1`.
    *   **State 2 (Computation Loop):**
        *   Continuously check if `A == 1`.
        *   If `A != 1`: Update `A = A / 2` and increment `L`.
        *   Loop until `A == 1`.

#### D. Key Concepts & Takeaways
*   **Bit-shifting:** An efficient hardware method for integer division by powers of two.
*   **FSM control:** Essential for managing the sequence of operations (initialization, data loading, iterative computation, termination).
*   **Component reuse:** The algorithm highlights how standard digital components can be combined for specific computational tasks.

---

### II. Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Algorithm

#### A. Key Concept
*   **Purpose:** Compute the Greatest Common Divisor of two integers, A and B, using an iterative subtraction method (Euclidean algorithm variant).

#### B. Sequential Algorithm
1.  Inputs: Integers `A` and `B`.
2.  **While `A` is not equal to `B`:**
    *   **If `A` is greater than `B`:**
        *   `A = A - B`
    *   **Else (`B` is greater than `A`):**
        *   `B = B - A`
3.  The final common value of `A` and `B` is the GCD.

#### C. Digital System Implementation
*   **Inputs:** Initial values for `A` and `B`.
*   **Components:**
    *   **Two Registers:** One for `A`, one for `B`, to store and update their values.
    *   **Two Subtractors:**
        *   One to compute `A - B`.
        *   One to compute `B - A`.
    *   **Multiplexers:** To route either the initial input data or the result of a subtraction back into the respective `A` or `B` registers.
    *   **Comparators:**
        *   **`A == B`:** To detect the termination condition of the algorithm.
        *   **`A > B`:** To determine which subtraction (`A-B` or `B-A`) should occur.
            *   **Optimization:** This can be determined by examining the Most Significant Bit (MSB) of the `A - B` result. If `A - B >= 0` (MSB `SAA` is `0` for unsigned or positive), then `A >= B`. If `A - B < 0` (MSB `SAA` is `1` for negative), then `A < B`.
*   **Finite State Machine (FSM) Control:**
    *   Manages the loading of initial `A` and `B`.
    *   Controls the `while` loop condition (`A != B`).
    *   Directs the conditional subtraction (`A-B` or `B-A`) based on the `A > B` comparison.
    *   Updates the correct register (`A` or `B`) with the new value.

#### D. Key Concepts & Takeaways
*   **Conditional Logic:** Hardware must implement `if-else` branching using comparators and multiplexers controlled by the FSM.
*   **Arithmetic Units:** Subtractors are fundamental building blocks for many algorithms.
*   **MSB for Comparison:** The sign bit (MSB) of a subtraction result can efficiently determine relative magnitudes (`A > B` or `A < B`).

---

### III. VHDL Implementation Considerations

#### A. Key Concepts
*   **Hardware Description Language (VHDL):** Used to describe and implement digital systems.
*   **Modularity:** Breaking down a complex system into smaller, manageable components.
*   **Structured Design:** Following established practices for writing VHDL code and organizing projects.

#### B. Important VHDL Practices (for Labs/Projects)
*   **Project Setup:**
    *   Create a new project in the chosen IDE (e.g., Vivado).
    *   **File Organization:** Crucially, *all VHDL source files should be placed in the project's root folder*. (Test benches are an exception, added as simulation sources). Avoid scatter/permission issues (e.g., with cloud storage folders like Dropbox).
*   **Component Instantiation:**
    *   **`generic map`:** Used to pass parameters (e.g., bit width for registers, counter range) to generic components, making them reusable.
    *   **`port map`:** Connects the signals of the instantiated component to the signals of the higher-level design.
*   **Finite State Machine (FSM) Coding:**
    *   **Recommended Method:** Use separate VHDL processes for:
        1.  **Transition Logic:** Determines the `next_state` based on the `current_state` and inputs.
        2.  **Output Logic:** Determines the outputs and register updates based on the `current_state`.
    *   (Refer to VHDL tutorial #6 for detailed examples).

#### C. Takeaways
*   **Root Folder Rule:** A critical practical tip for avoiding common VHDL project compilation and permission issues.
*   **Parameterization:** `generic map` is powerful for creating flexible and reusable hardware components.
*   **Structured FSM:** A disciplined approach to FSM coding improves readability, maintainability, and correctness.
*   **Practical Skills:** Emphasizes the translation of theoretical algorithms and block diagrams into actual VHDL code for FPGA implementation.

---

## Exam-Ready Summary: Digital System Design Principles

To design digital systems from sequential algorithms, a systematic approach is required, leveraging Finite State Machines (FSMs) for control and fundamental digital components for data manipulation.

1.  **Algorithm to Hardware Translation:**
    *   Break down the sequential algorithm into discrete steps and conditions.
    *   Map data storage to **Registers**.
    *   Map iterative processes to **Counters** or register updates within loops.
    *   Map arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, division/shift) to dedicated **Arithmetic Logic Units (ALUs)** or specific combinational logic.
    *   Map conditional branches (if-else, while loops) to **Comparators** and **Multiplexers** for data routing, all controlled by an **FSM**.

2.  **Key Digital Components:**
    *   **Registers:** Store data (`A`, `B`, `L`).
    *   **Counters:** Increment/decrement values (e.g., for `L`).
    *   **Subtractors:** Perform `A-B` or `B-A`.
    *   **Comparators:** Evaluate conditions like `A==1`, `A==B`, `A>B`.
    *   **Multiplexers:** Select data paths (e.g., load initial value or updated value into a register).
    *   **Shifters:** Efficiently perform division/multiplication by powers of two.

3.  **Illustrative Algorithms:**
    *   **Integer Base-2 Logarithm:**
        *   **Algorithm:** `L=0`; `while A!=1: A = A/2 (floor), L=L+1`.
        *   **Hardware Mapping:** `A` in a register, `L` in a counter. `A/2` is a bit right-shift. `A==1` check is a comparator/NOR gate. FSM controls the loop.
    *   **Greatest Common Divisor (GCD):**
        *   **Algorithm:** `while A!=B: if A>B: A=A-B else: B=B-A`.
        *   **Hardware Mapping:** `A` and `B` in registers. Two subtractors for `A-B` and `B-A`. Comparators for `A==B` (loop termination) and `A>B` (conditional subtraction, often using the MSB of `A-B`). FSM orchestrates the conditional updates.

4.  **VHDL Implementation Best Practices:**
    *   **Modularity:** Design components (e.g., FSM, counter, shifter) separately.
    *   **Component Instantiation:** Use `generic map` for parameterization and `port map` for signal connections.
    *   **FSM Coding:** Implement FSMs using separate processes for state transitions and output generation for clarity and robustness.
    *   **Project Structure:** Place all VHDL source files (except test benches) in the root directory of the project to avoid common build issues.

**Overall Takeaway:** Understanding how to break down an algorithm, identify the necessary hardware components, and orchestrate their operation with an FSM is fundamental to digital system design. Efficient hardware implementations often involve leveraging specific bit-level operations and careful consideration of control flow.

# EC 4710: Computer Hardware Design – Course Introduction

## I. Course Overview

*   **Course Name:** Computer Hardware Design
*   **Course ID:** EC 4710
*   **Instructor:** Professor [Name not explicitly stated, but contact info on screen]
    *   **Office Hours:** Tuesdays 2:00 PM - 4:00 PM (virtual). Flexible appointments available upon request.
*   **Lecture Format:**
    *   **Tuesdays:** Asynchronous (pre-recorded videos posted), typically theory-focused (first lecture is an exception).
    *   **Thursdays:** Synchronous (live sessions), typically focused on examples.

## II. Labs & Logistics

*   **Lab Sections:** Four sections total.
    *   Only one section (007 or 15707) is online.
    *   All other sections are face-to-face.
*   **Lab Schedule & Concerns:**
    *   Face-to-face labs are scheduled to start in the third week.
    *   **Warning:** Professor suspects all labs may ultimately move online for the entire semester due to ongoing circumstances.
    *   Online labs are considered less effective for this subject ("don't work really well").
*   **TA Support:** TAs are available weekly for assistance starting January 19th (if labs are in-person).
*   **Online Lab Capacity:** Limited to 10 students, but not expected to be reached.

## III. Course Materials & Tools

*   **Primary Resources:**
    *   All course materials (notes) will be posted on **Moodle**.
    *   A backup link will also be provided.
    *   **Crucial:** VHEL tutorial, specifically units 7, 8, and 9. This class has a strong VHDL design focus.
*   **Recommended Textbooks:**
    *   **Not required.** Course content will be covered in notes.
    *   **Recommended (for examples):** Poncho's book (focuses on useful digital system examples).
    *   Other books are available for theory, but theory will be covered in class.
*   **Required Hardware:**
    *   **FPGA Board (e.g., Nexys 4 DDR or equivalent):** Absolutely essential. Students should already own this from EC 2700. If not, purchase immediately.
    *   **Do NOT rely on lab boards** due to limited access (3 hours/week) and potential for fully online labs.
*   **Required Software:**
    *   **Vivado Webpack Edition:** Any recent version (2017, 2018, 2019) is acceptable.
    *   **MATLAB:** Available remotely via desktop service, or use a personal license.
    *   **Octave:** Open-source alternative to MATLAB.
    *   **Note:** Vivado servers cannot program physical boards remotely.

## IV. Course Objectives (Key Concepts)

Students will learn to:

*   Implement combinational and sequential components using **VHDL**.
*   Connect VHDL components to create functional **digital systems**.
*   Design **custom architectures** for interacting with external peripherals.
*   Develop **special-purpose processors** (advanced digital systems).
*   Create robust **test benches** for digital systems.
*   Perform extensive **simulation** of digital designs.
*   Collaborate in teams on a **final project**.
*   **Overall Focus:** More on design and practice (VHDL) than pure theory.

## V. Grading & Assignments

*   **Grading Scheme (Brief):**
    *   Homeworks
    *   **Labs (36%):** This is the largest component, emphasizing hands-on digital design and VHDL.
    *   Midterm Exam
    *   Final Project
    *   **No quizzes or final exam.**
*   **Homework Policies:**
    *   Typically posted for one week.
    *   **Strict Deadline:** No late submissions accepted. Submit online by midnight on the due date.
    *   Involve writing and simulating digital circuit code (no board programming required for homework).
*   **Midterm Exam:**
    *   **Date:** February 18th.
    *   **Format:** Online, closed notes. Students will work independently. Professor will be available via Zoom for questions.
    *   Most students typically perform well.

## I. Administrative & Logistics

*   **Lab Access**:
    *   Restricted to scheduled lab times only.
    *   TA will be present during scheduled times.
    *   Building access expected to be open by the 19th (subject to change).
    *   Previously, open lab access was allowed, but no longer due to COVID restrictions.
*   **Subject Components**:
    *   First unit posted in Moodle: "Digital System Design."
*   **Grading Criteria**: Not discussed in this segment of the lecture.

## II. Introduction to Abstraction Layers in Computer Systems Design

*   **Core Question**: Why learn digital system design (e.g., VHDL/Verilog) when software alternatives (like Arduino) exist?
*   **Answer**: Different implementation approaches offer various trade-offs in performance, resource usage, and development effort. This class focuses on designing at lower abstraction layers.
*   **Concept**: Computer systems can be built using multiple "construction" or "abstraction" layers, ranging from high-level software to low-level hardware.

## III. Implementation Approaches: A Spectrum of Abstraction

The choice of implementation layer depends on specific system constraints and requirements.

### A. Software Implementations (Higher Abstraction)

1.  **MATLAB/Simulink**:
    *   **Pros**:
        *   Very easy to write code, often no explicit variable declaration.
        *   Simulink allows drag-and-drop component assembly.
        *   Fastest development time.
    *   **Cons**:
        *   Slower execution speed.
        *   Consumes more memory.
        *   Less efficient.
2.  **C/C++**:
    *   **Pros**:
        *   Faster execution than MATLAB (assuming good programming practices).
        *   More memory efficient.
    *   **Cons**:
        *   Requires more skilled programming.
        *   Longer development time compared to MATLAB.
3.  **Embedded C (Microprocessor/Microcontroller - e.g., Arduino)**:
    *   **Pros**:
        *   Better performance and efficiency than general C/C++ on a PC.
        *   A common stopping point for many projects.
    *   **Cons**:
        *   Requires even more skillful programming techniques.

### B. Hardware Implementations (Lower Abstraction / Digital System Design)

1.  **Digital System Design (VHDL/Verilog)**:
    *   Involves designing the system at a hardware level using Hardware Description Languages (HDLs).
    *   **Levels**:
        *   **System Level**: Using common, pre-built functional blocks.
        *   **Register Transfer Level (RTL)**: Designing with registers, counters, multiplexers, and other fundamental digital logic components. (This is a primary focus of the course).
    *   **Pros**:
        *   Much, much faster execution speed.
        *   Significantly less memory consumption.
        *   Lower power consumption (critical for many applications).
    *   **Cons**:
        *   Far more challenging and difficult to design.
        *   Requires specialized knowledge of VHDL/Verilog and proper simulation techniques.
        *   Longer development and debugging cycles.
2.  **Logic Gate Level**:
    *   Designing directly with basic logic gates (AND, OR, NOT, etc.).
    *   **Pros**: Potentially even finer-grained optimization.
    *   **Cons**: Extremely annoying and complex to implement for non-trivial systems.
3.  **Transistor Level**:
    *   Designing the system using individual transistors.
    *   **Pros**:
        *   Provides the absolute best performance and highest level of optimization.
        *   Used for modern microprocessors.
    *   **Cons**:
        *   Requires highly specialized and expensive engineering skills.
        *   Very costly and time-consuming development.

## IV. Application-Specific Choices & Examples

*   **Decision Factor**: The "best" implementation depends entirely on the application's constraints and priorities (e.g., speed, power, cost, development time, production volume).
*   **Examples**:
    *   **Christmas Lights**: Implemented easily with an Arduino (embedded C) due to low performance requirements and ease of development. No need for complex VHDL.
    *   **Video Codec (H.265)**: Requires extremely high performance for real-time video compression/decompression. Software implementations are often too slow.
        *   Typically implemented using **Application-Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs)**, which are pure hardware chips designed for this specific function.
        *   ASICs are designed using VHDL/Verilog at lower abstraction levels to achieve necessary speed and efficiency.
    *   **Temperature Sensor Chip / I²C Interface**:
        *   Commercial temperature sensors (like those on Nexus boards) are tiny chips designed in pure hardware.
        *   The I²C communication logic within microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino's ATmega chip) is also implemented in **pure hardware**, not software, to ensure reliable and efficient bit-level operations. This is why it's a "peripheral" of the microcontroller.
*   **Conclusion**: For mass-produced chips or highly demanding applications (millions of units, extreme performance/efficiency), pure digital logic (hardware) design is often the only viable option.

---

## V. Key Concepts

*   **Abstraction Layers**: Hierarchical levels of detail in system design, from high-level software down to transistor-level hardware.
*   **Trade-offs**: Every design choice involves balancing performance (speed), resource consumption (memory, power), and development effort (skill, time, cost).
*   **Hardware Description Languages (HDLs)**: VHDL and Verilog are used to describe digital circuits, enabling design at the Register Transfer Level and below.
*   **ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit)**: A custom chip designed for a particular application, often leveraging pure hardware for maximum performance and efficiency.

## VI. Important Takeaways (Exam-Ready Summary)

*   **Understand the Spectrum**: Be able to identify and differentiate between software (MATLAB, C/C++, Embedded C) and hardware (RTL/VHDL, logic gates, transistors) implementation approaches.
*   **Know the Trade-offs**: For each major abstraction layer, articulate its primary advantages (e.g., speed, memory, power, development time) and disadvantages.
    *   *Software*: Faster development, easier, but slower and more resource-intensive.
    *   *Hardware*: Faster execution, less memory/power, but much harder, longer development, requires specialized skills.
*   **Context is Key**: The "best" implementation is not universal but is dictated by specific application requirements and constraints (performance, cost, power budget, production volume).
*   **Role of VHDL/Digital Design**: This course (Digital System Design) focuses on the hardware design aspect (especially RTL with VHDL), which is critical for high-performance, low-power, and mass-market digital systems where software solutions are inadequate.

# Digital System Design: Levels, Architecture, & Components

## 1. Digital System Implementation Levels

Understanding the various levels of digital system implementation helps in design and debugging. These levels range from low-level hardware to high-level software abstraction.

### Key Concepts: Implementation Levels

*   **Register Transfer Level (RTL)**:
    *   Focus: Uses specific hardware components like registers, multiplexers, decoders, and shift registers.
    *   **Class Focus**: This class primarily operates at the RTL level.
*   **Micro-architecture Level**:
    *   Focus: System-level hardware design; implementing and interconnecting blocks.
    *   **Class Coverage**: This class also covers some aspects of micro-architecture design (e.g., microprocessors).
*   **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) Level**:
    *   Focus: Designing the set of instructions for a microprocessor to optimize application performance. This bridges hardware and software.
    *   **Class Coverage**: Briefly touches upon instruction set design.
*   **Operating System Level**:
    *   Focus: Design and implementation of operating systems (from bare-bones to complex).
*   **Programming Language Level**:
    *   Focus: Using languages like C, Python, MATLAB (or interpreters). Different tiers exist within programming languages.
*   **Algorithm Level**:
    *   Focus: The ideal state where systems could directly implement algorithms from high-level descriptions. Currently, we are not fully there.

### Important Distinction

*   **Hardware Implementation**: Primarily RTL and Micro-architecture levels.
*   **Software Implementation**: Mostly Operating System, Programming Language, and Algorithm levels.
*   **Note**: These levels are not monolithic; mixing elements is common.

## 2. Digital System Architecture

A digital system typically consists of two main interconnected parts.

### Key Concepts: System Structure

*   **State Machine (Controller)**:
    *   Usually one, but can be more.
    *   Controls the overall circuit operation.
*   **Data Path Circuit**:
    *   Performs data processing operations.
    *   Can be simple or highly complex, containing many components and even other state machines.
*   **Interactions**:
    *   System inputs can feed both the state machine and the data path.
    *   Data path outputs can control the state machine.
    *   The state machine can control the data path.

## 3. Essential Knowledge for Digital System Design (Class Focus)

To succeed in this course and digital system design, specific skills are crucial.

### Core Skills

*   **Finite State Machines (FSMs)**:
    *   Understanding their concept and behavior.
    *   **ASM diagrams** are particularly important and will be used extensively in labs and homeworks.
*   **Component Interconnection**:
    *   Ability to connect various components like multiplexers, decoders, and registers.
*   **Hierarchical Design**:
    *   Designing systems using multiple interconnected components.
*   **VHDL Description**:
    *   Describing all parts of the system using Hardware Description Languages (HDL).
*   **Data Path Circuit Usage**:
    *   Proficiency in using and designing data path circuits.

## 4. Common Data Path Components & VHDL Libraries

Certain components are frequently used in digital system design. Re-implementing them from scratch is inefficient and error-prone.

### Key Concepts: Reusable Components

*   **Common Components**:
    *   Counters
    *   Registers (including shift registers, parallel access shift registers)
    *   Multiplexers
    *   Decoders
    *   Arithmetic Units (adders, multipliers)
*   **Importance of Reusability**:
    *   Counters, registers, and shift registers are extremely popular.
    *   It's more efficient and less error-prone to use **pre-designed VHDL components** (like a library).
*   **Provided Parametric VHDL Components**:
    *   `my_reg_E`: N-bit register with enabled and asynchronous clear.
    *   `my_counter_Mod_N`: Modulo N counter with enabled and synchronous clear.
    *   `my_DA_shift_register`: Parallel access (left/right) shift register with enabled and synchronous clear.
    *   **Parametric Design**: These components are *parametric*, meaning you can specify parameters like bit-width (N) using `generic map` in VHDL. (Refer to VHDL for FPGA tutorial #7).
    *   **Recommendation**: Use these components extensively to avoid creating new errors and save time.

## 5. Examples of Digital Systems

Understanding practical applications helps solidify theoretical concepts.

### Examples

*   **Car Load Counter**:
    *   **Function**: Counts cars entering/leaving a parking lot.
    *   **Architecture**:
        *   Complex State Machine: Handles sequences of sensor inputs.
        *   Simple Data Path: A counter (increments for entry, decrements for exit).
    *   **Representations**: Uses both normal state machine diagrams and ASM diagrams (the preferred method for this class).
*   **Debouncing Circuit**:
    *   **Problem**: Mechanical switches (push buttons) generate noisy, "bouncing" electrical signals when pressed, not a clean transition.
    *   **Goal**: Filter the noisy input to produce a stable, clean signal.
    *   **Solution Approach**: Assign a logic value (0 or 1) only after the input signal has remained constant for a minimum duration (e.g., 20 milliseconds), effectively ignoring transient bounces.
    *   **Architecture**: Involves a State Machine and a Counter (e.g., a modular counter with a comparator) to measure the stable duration.

# Lecture Summary: Digital System Design & Component Behavior

## 1. Signal Debouncing Circuit (Example)

### Key Concept
*   **Debouncing:** Eliminating spurious signal transitions (noise) by ensuring a signal remains stable for a minimum duration (e.g., 20ms) before its value is registered. This prevents multiple unwanted triggers from a single physical event (like a button press).

### Operation Overview
*   **Goal:** To produce a stable debounced output (`W_DBV`) from a noisy input signal (`W`).
*   **State 1 (Wait for '1'):**
    *   `W_DBV` is 0.
    *   Clear an internal counter.
    *   Wait for input `W` to become '1'.
    *   **If** `W` becomes '1': Start incrementing the counter.
    *   **Condition Check:** If `W` returns to '0' *before* the counter reaches the 20ms threshold, reset the counter and stay in State 1.
    *   **Transition:** If `W` remains '1' for *at least* 20ms (counter reaches threshold), transition to State 2, and set `W_DBV = 1`.
*   **State 2 (Wait for '0'):**
    *   `W_DBV` is 1.
    *   Clear an internal counter.
    *   Wait for input `W` to become '0'.
    *   **If** `W` becomes '0': Start incrementing the counter.
    *   **Condition Check:** If `W` returns to '1' *before* the counter reaches the 20ms threshold, reset the counter and stay in State 2.
    *   **Transition:** If `W` remains '0' for *at least* 20ms (counter reaches threshold), transition back to State 1, and set `W_DBV = 0`.

### Important Takeaway
*   This process demonstrates the critical role of **counters** and **Algorithmic State Machine (ASM) diagrams** in implementing sequential logic that relies on timing and state management.

---

## 2. Bit Counting Circuit Design (Example)

### Objective
*   To count the total number of '1's present in a given sequence of bits (e.g., an 8-bit input 'A').
    *   *Example:* For input `A = 10101011`, the output `count` should be 5.

### Sequential Algorithm (Conceptual)
1.  Initialize `count = 0`.
2.  **While** input `A` is not equal to zero:
    *   **If** the Least Significant Bit (LSB) of `A` is '1', then increment `count` (`count = count + 1`).
    *   Perform a right shift on `A` (`A = A >> 1`) to move the next bit to the LSB position.
3.  The loop terminates when `A` becomes zero, and `count` holds the final result.

### Digital System Design Methodology

#### A. High-Level State Machine (HLSM)
*   **State 0 (Idle):**
    *   Initialize `count = 0`.
    *   Wait for a `Start` signal (input).
*   **State 1 (Load Data):**
    *   When `Start = 1`, load the input bit sequence `A` into an internal register.
*   **State 2 (Process Bits):**
    *   Repeatedly perform the algorithm's core steps: check `LSB(A)`, potentially increment `count`, and right-shift `A`.
    *   Stay in this state until `A` becomes 0.
*   **State 3 (Done):**
    *   When `A = 0`, processing is complete.
    *   Assert a `Done` signal (output) to indicate `count` is ready.
    *   Wait for `Start` to become '0' before returning to State 0 (Idle) for a new operation.

#### B. Implementation (ASM Diagram + Data Path)
*   **Key Hardware Components (Data Path):**
    *   **Parallel Access Shift Register:** To load the initial `A` value in parallel and perform subsequent right shifts.
    *   **Counter:** To keep track of the number of '1's found.
    *   **Comparator (or NOR gate):** To detect when `A` (the shifted data) becomes 0.
    *   **LSB Extractor:** To get the least significant bit of `A`.
*   **Control Unit (State Machine):**
    *   Generated from the HLSM, this component controls all data path elements using specific control signals (e.g., `ClrC` to clear the counter, `Ea` to enable loading/shifting the register, `Ec` to enable counter increment).
    *   State transitions are driven by inputs like `Start`, `A_is_0`, and `LSB(A)`.
*   **System Interface:** The system takes a `Start` signal and `A` as inputs, and provides `count` and a `Done` signal as outputs.

---

## 3. General Digital System Design Approach

### Methodological Steps
1.  **Algorithm to High-Level State Machine (HLSM):** Start by conceptualizing the algorithm's flow into distinct states and transitions, outlining major operations in each state.
2.  **HLSM to ASM Diagram + Data Path:** Translate the HLSM into a detailed Algorithmic State Machine (ASM) diagram. This involves identifying necessary hardware components (the "Data Path") and defining the specific control signals the state machine will generate to orchestrate their operations.

### Standard System Interface
*   Most digital systems utilize a **`Start` signal** to initiate an operation and a **`Done` signal** to indicate that the output data is valid and ready.

---

## 4. Essential Knowledge & Skills for Digital Design

### Key Concepts
*   **Common Component Behavior:** A deep understanding of how fundamental digital components operate is crucial:
    *   **Counters:** How to enable them, clear them, and cause them to increment (refer to course materials, e.g., "Notes 7").
    *   **Registers:** How to load data into them (parallel load) and enable their operation.
    *   **Shift Registers:** How to load data and perform shift operations (left or right).
*   **ASM Diagrams:** Proficiency in reading, interpreting, and designing with Algorithmic State Machine diagrams is fundamental for representing and implementing sequential logic.

### Important Skills
*   **Timing Diagram Completion:** The ability to accurately analyze and complete timing diagrams is an indispensable skill for debugging and verifying the behavior of complex circuits, especially as systems grow more intricate.
*   **Practice:** Implementing digital systems is a practical skill that develops over time through hands-on experience and exposure to a variety of design examples. There is no single "straightforward" method that works for every scenario.

*   **Bit Counting Circuit:** An example of digital system design. Algorithmically counts '1's by checking the LSB and right-shifting an input `A` until `A` is zero, storing the count in a counter.

# Digital System Design: Algorithmic Implementations

## 1. Integer Base-2 Logarithm Algorithm

### Key Concepts
*   **Sequential Algorithm:** An iterative process to compute the integer base-2 logarithm of a number `A`.
*   **Truncation/Rounding Down:** Essential for the division by 2 operation.

### Algorithm Steps
*   Initialize `L = 0`.
*   While `A` is not equal to `1`:
    *   Update `A = A / 2` (truncate/round towards zero).
    *   Increment `L` by `1`.
*   The final value of `L` is the integer base-2 logarithm.

### Hardware Implementation Components
*   **L (Output):** Implemented using a **Counter**.
*   **A (Input Data):** Stored in a **Register**.
*   **`A / 2` Operation:** Achieved by **right-shifting bits** (e.g., A3 A2 A1 A0 becomes 0 A3 A2 A1). This naturally performs division by 2 with truncation (Florian operator).
*   **`A != 1` Check:** Can be implemented with a **Comparator** or, more efficiently, by checking if `A/2` (the right-shifted value) is `0` using a **NOR gate**.
*   **Control Logic:** A **Finite State Machine (FSM)** manages the sequence:
    *   **State 1 (Initialization):** Set `L = 0`, Load initial `D_A` into register `A`.
    *   **State 2 (Computation Loop):** Repeatedly update `A` and increment `L` until `A = 1`.
*   **Multiplexer:** Routes either the initial input `D_A` or the updated `A` (from the shift operation) back to the `A` register.

## 2. GCD (Greatest Common Divisor) Algorithm

### Key Concepts
*   **Euclidean Algorithm Variant:** An iterative subtraction-based method to find the GCD of two numbers.

### Algorithm Steps
*   Given two integers `A` and `B`.
*   While `A` is not equal to `B`:
    *   If `A > B`, then `A = A - B`.
    *   Else (`B > A`), then `B = B - A`.
*   The final value of `A` (or `B`, since they are equal) is the GCD.

### Hardware Implementation Components
*   **A & B (Inputs):** Stored in two separate **Registers**.
*   **Subtraction (`A - B`, `B - A`):** Implemented using two **Subtractor** units.
*   **Updating Registers:** Multiplexers select the output of the appropriate subtractor to load back into register `A` or `B`.
*   **Initial Data Loading:** Multiplexers also allow loading initial external data `D_A` and `D_B` into the registers.
*   **`A == B` Check:** Implemented using a **Comparator**.
*   **`A > B` Check:** Can be determined by examining the **Most Significant Bit (MSB)** of the `A - B` result.
    *   If `MSB(A-B) = 0`, then `A - B >= 0` (so `A >= B`).
    *   If `MSB(A-B) = 1`, then `A - B < 0` (so `A < B`). This uses computer arithmetic properties.
*   **Control Logic:** An **FSM** orchestrates the sequential steps, based on the comparator outputs.

## 3. VHDL Implementation Principles (Quick Overview)

### Key Principles for Digital System Design in VHDL
*   **File Organization:** Place all component VHDL files in the **root project folder**. Test bench files are typically categorized as "test sources."
*   **Component Instantiation:**
    *   Use `generic map` for configurable parameters (e.g., `WIDTH` for registers, `MAX_COUNT` for counters).
    *   Use `port map` to connect signals between components.
*   **Finite State Machine (FSM) Design:**
    *   A recommended approach uses distinct processes for **state transitions** and **output logic**. (Refer to VHDL tutorial #6 for details).
*   **Debugging Tip:** Issues with file permissions (e.g., in cloud sync folders like Dropbox) can prevent project creation/compilation. Using a local root directory (e.g., `D:\`) can resolve this.

---

## Important Takeaways

*   **Algorithm to Hardware Mapping:** Any sequential algorithm can be translated into a digital system using a combination of registers, combinational logic (subtractors, comparators, shifters, gates), and a Finite State Machine (FSM) for control.
*   **Reusability:** Components like counters, registers, and basic arithmetic units are fundamental building blocks.
*   **Efficiency:** Clever bit manipulation (like right-shifting for A/2) and arithmetic properties (MSB for comparison) can lead to optimized hardware implementations.
*   **Systematic Design:** A structured approach (algorithm -> high-level FSM -> component mapping -> VHDL implementation) is crucial for complex designs.

## Exam-Ready Summary

Digital systems are designed by translating sequential algorithms into hardware using **Finite State Machines (FSMs)** for control and various digital components for data manipulation. Key components include **registers** (for storing data), **counters** (for incrementing/decrementing values), **subtractors**, **comparators**, **multiplexers** (for data routing), and basic **logic gates** (e.g., NOR for zero checks). The **Integer Base-2 Logarithm** and **GCD** algorithms demonstrate this mapping: both use iterative processes (controlled by FSM states) and involve arithmetic operations (like right-shifting for division or subtraction) and comparisons (`A==1`, `A==B`, `A>B`). Notably, division by two can be done by bit-shifting, and magnitude comparison (`A>B`) can be inferred from the MSB of `A-B`. In **VHDL**, proper project structure (files in root) and accurate component instantiation (`generic map` for parameters, `port map` for connections) are essential for successful implementation.
